sentences.chinese: 六歲，坐與京房論議免，語在《房傳》。
sentences.literal: Within six years he sat for discussing and debating with Jing Fang of the capital and was dismissed; the account is in Fang's biography.
sentences.idiomatic: Six years later he lost his post for debating policy with Jing Fang of the capital; the story is told in Jing Fang's biography.

sentences.chinese: 贊曰：所謂鹽鐵議者，起始元中，徵文學賢良問以治亂，皆對願罷郡國鹽鐵、酒榷均輸，務本抑末，毋與天下爭利，然後教化可興。
sentences.literal: The appraisal says: What is called the salt-and-iron debate began in the Shiyuan era, when men of letters and worthy candidates were summoned and asked about order and disorder; all replied that they wished to abolish commandery and kingdom monopolies on salt and iron, wine excise, and equal-delivery transport, to devote effort to the root and check the branch, not to contend for profit with all under Heaven—only then could transformation be raised.
sentences.idiomatic: The historian's verdict: the salt-and-iron debate opened in the Shiyuan years, when the court summoned literati and worthies and asked how to cure the realm's ills. They answered in one voice—scrap the state monopolies on salt, iron, wine, and the equal-supply caravans, strengthen agriculture and starve the merchant, stop competing with the people for coin—only then could moral instruction take root.

sentences.chinese: 御史大夫弘羊以為此乃所以安邊竟，制四夷，國家大業，不可廢也。
sentences.literal: Imperial Counselor Hongyang thought that this was the means whereby to secure the border marches, control the four barbarians, and accomplish the state's great enterprise—it could not be abolished.
sentences.idiomatic: Imperial Counselor Sang Hongyang countered that those policies guarded the frontier, cowed the four quarters, and underpinned the dynasty—they could not simply be junked.

sentences.chinese: 當時相詰難，頗有其議文。
sentences.literal: At the time they cross-examined one another; there are rather full texts of their arguments.
sentences.idiomatic: The two sides cross-examined each other at length, and much of that dialogue survives.

sentences.chinese: 至宣帝時，汝南桓寬次公治《公羊春秋》舉為郎，至廬江太守丞，博通善屬文，推衍鹽鐵之議，增廣條目，極其論難，著數萬言，亦欲以究治亂，成一家之法焉。
sentences.literal: By Emperor Xuandi's time Huan Kuan of Runan, courtesy name Cigong, who studied the Gongyang commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals and had been raised to gentleman, reached assistant to the Guardian of Lujiang; broadly learned and skilled at composition, he expanded the salt-and-iron debate, widened the entries, carried argument and rebuttal to the limit, and wrote several tens of thousands of characters, also wishing thereby to investigate order and disorder and complete a school of method of his own.
sentences.idiomatic: Under Xuandi, Huan Kuan of Runan—who had mastered the Gongyang Annals, risen to gentleman, and risen to aide to the governor of Lujiang—was erudite and eloquent. He reworked the debate into a book, added topics, pushed every argument to its limit, and wrote tens of thousands of characters, hoping to settle what brings order and what brings chaos in a treatise that stands on its own.

sentences.chinese: 其辭曰：「觀公卿賢良文學之議，『異乎吾所聞』。
sentences.literal: Its wording says: 'Observing the debate of the dukes, ministers, worthy candidates, and men of letters—"it differs from what I have heard."'
sentences.idiomatic: His preface reads: "Reading the speeches of ministers and worthies, I find them far from what I had been taught."

sentences.chinese: 聞汝南硃生言，當此之時，英俊並進，賢良茂陵唐生、文學魯國萬生之徒六十有餘人咸聚闕庭，舒六藝之風，陳治平之原，知者贊其慮，仁者明其施，勇者見其斷，辯者騁其辭，斷斷焉，行行焉，雖未詳備，斯可略觀矣。
sentences.literal: Hearing what Master Zhu of Runan said—at that time the talented advanced together; more than sixty such as the worthy Tang Sheng of Maoling, the man of letters Wan Sheng of Lu, and their like all gathered in the palace court, spreading the airs of the six arts, setting forth the sources of peace and order; the wise praised their plans, the benevolent clarified their measures, the brave showed their decisiveness, the eloquent gave rein to their words—decidedly, resolutely, though not exhaustively detailed, thereby one may broadly observe.
sentences.idiomatic: Runan's Master Zhu told me how, in that hall, talent pressed forward together: more than sixty worthies—Tang Sheng of Maoling, Wan Sheng of Lu, and the rest—thronged the court, unfurled the six classics, traced the springs of good government. Wisdom applauded their designs, humanity their policies, courage their resolve, rhetoric their language. Sharp, steadfast, if not complete—it is still a panorama worth studying.

sentences.chinese: 中山劉子推言王道，撟當世，反諸正，彬彬然弘博君子也。
sentences.literal: Liu Zitui of Zhongshan discoursed on the kingly Way, straightened what was awry in the age, returned matters to the correct norm—truly a cultivated, broad-minded gentleman.
sentences.idiomatic: Liu Zitui of Zhongshan preached the royal Way, bent the age back toward rectitude, and carried himself like a true junzi—learned, ample, composed.

sentences.chinese: 九江祝生奮史魚之節，發憤懣，譏公卿，介然直而不撓，可謂不畏強圉矣。
sentences.literal: Zhu Sheng of Jiujiang roused the integrity of Ziyu of Wei, vented indignant resentment, satirized the dukes and ministers, stood firm and straight without bending—he may be called one who did not fear the mighty.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhu Sheng of Jiujiang took up the unbending spirit of Ziyu of Wei, poured out honest rage, mocked the high ministers, and would not bow—here was a man who feared no power.

sentences.chinese: 桑大夫據當世，合時變，上權利之略，雖非正當，巨儒宿學不能自解，博物通達之士也。
sentences.literal: Minister Sang grasped the present age, fitted the changes of the times, offered stratagems of power and profit; though not orthodox, great scholars and long-studied men could not untangle themselves—he was a man broadly learned and penetrating.
sentences.idiomatic: Minister Sang spoke to his own day, adapted to shifting circumstance, and spun schemes of profit and control. His doctrine was not classical, yet the greatest academicians could not refute him—whatever else, he was formidably learned.

sentences.chinese: 然攝公卿之柄，不師古始，放於末利，處非其位，行非其道，果隕其性，以及厥宗。
sentences.literal: Yet he seized the handle of the dukes and ministers, did not model himself on antiquity at the beginning, gave rein to branch profits, occupied a position not his, pursued a Way not his—in the end he ruined himself and reached to his clan.
sentences.idiomatic: Yet he seized the levers of state, scorned the models of antiquity, chased the merchant's margin, sat in a chair that did not fit him, and walked a path that was not his—until he destroyed himself and dragged his kin down with him.

sentences.chinese: 車丞相履伊、呂之列，當軸處中，括囊不言，容身而去，彼哉！
sentences.literal: The Cart Chancellor trod the ranks of Yi Yin and Lü Wang, sat on the axle at the center, tied the sack shut and did not speak, preserved his person and departed—such men!
sentences.idiomatic: Che Qianqiu stood where Yi Yin and Lü Wang once stood, yet at the hub of power he sealed his lips like a tied purse, never counseled aloud, saved his skin, and withdrew—such men!

sentences.chinese: 若夫丞相、御史兩府之士，不能正議以輔宰相，成同類，長同行，阿意苟合，以說其上，『斗筲之徒，何足選也！』」
sentences.literal: As for the gentlemen of the two bureaus of chancellor and Imperial Counselor, who could not set right debate to assist the chancellor, who formed cliques of the same kind, lengthened lines of the same walk, bent their intent to easy agreement to please those above—'fellows of the pint and peck basket—how could they be worth selecting!'"
sentences.idiomatic: And the clerks of chancellor and censor yamens who dared not speak straight to aid their chief, who flocked with their own kind, lengthened each other's faults, and flattered upward for favor—"those pint-measure politicians were never worth the choosing!"
